messages.content: Add an FAQ section with 4 common questions people ask before starting a vending machine business.

context.title: Coffee vending for offices
context.metaDescription: Coffee vending machines for modern workplaces.

props.heading: Old headline
props.body: Coffee vending machines for offices keep teams stocked with fresh drinks without anyone owning a brewing rota. A managed machine covers restocking, cleaning, and fault response so facilities can stay focused on the building.

input.title: Starting a Vending Business FAQ
input.body: Find answers to the most common questions about launching and managing a commercial vending route, from capital requirements to site selection.

faqItems.question: How much capital is needed to start a vending machine business?
faqItems.answer: Initial costs vary based on whether you buy new or used equipment. Starting a small route typically requires between three thousand and ten thousand dollars. This covers the physical machines, initial inventory stock, transport, and any local business licensing fees.

faqItems.question: How do you secure profitable locations for your machines?
faqItems.answer: High foot traffic is the primary factor for success. You should draft a professional proposal for property owners or office managers. Many operators offer a small percentage of sales or provide the service as a staff benefit to secure these spots.

faqItems.question: What are the ongoing maintenance and restocking requirements?
faqItems.answer: Most successful operators visit their machines once or twice a week depending on volume. Regular tasks include replenishing snack or beverage stock, cleaning touchpoints, testing payment systems, and pulling cash. Keeping a consistent schedule prevents empty shelves and lost revenue.

faqItems.question: Should I focus on specialty coffee or traditional snack machines?
faqItems.answer: Coffee vending machines for offices offer high margins but require water line access and regular sanitization. Traditional snack systems are simpler to maintain but have lower individual transaction values. Many new business owners start with a mix to balance maintenance time against profit margins.
